   902→=== L09_IntersectionIdempotency.lean ===
   903→import Game.Metadata
   904→
   905→World "FinsetOperations"
   906→Level 9
   907→
   908→Title "Intersection Idempotency"
   909→
   910→Introduction "
   911→# Your First ext Identity: s ∩ s = s
   912→
   913→The simplest set identity you can prove with `ext`: intersecting a
   914→set with itself gives back the same set.
   915→
   916→After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_inter]`, the goal becomes:
   917→$$x \\in s \\land x \\in s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
   918→
   919→**New notation**: `.1` and `.2` on conjunction hypotheses.
   920→
   921→When you have `h : P ∧ Q`, you can extract:
   922→- `h.1` gives the first component (`P`)
   923→- `h.2` gives the second component (`Q`)
   924→
   925→And to *build* a conjunction, use anonymous constructors:
   926→`⟨proof_of_P, proof_of_Q⟩` is shorthand for `And.intro proof_of_P proof_of_Q`.
   927→
   928→**Forward**: given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, project with `h.1`.
   929→
   930→**Backward**: given `hs : x ∈ s`, duplicate with `⟨hs, hs⟩`.
   931→
   932→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cap s = s$.
   933→"
   934→
   935→/-- Intersection is idempotent: s ∩ s = s. -/
   936→Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ s = s := by
   937→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
   938→  membership biconditional."
   939→  ext x
   940→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to unfold the intersection."
   941→  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
   942→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ s`.
   943→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
   944→  constructor
   945→  · Hint "Given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s`.
   946→    Use `intro h` and then `exact h.1` to project the first component."
   947→    intro h
   948→    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h.1` — the `.1` projection extracts
   949→    the first component of the conjunction."
   950→    exact h.1
   951→  · Hint "Given `hs : x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`.
   952→    Use `intro hs` and then `exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` to build the conjunction."
   953→    intro hs
   954→    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` — the anonymous constructor
   955→    builds the conjunction by duplicating the same proof."
   956→    exact ⟨hs, hs⟩
   957→
   958→Conclusion "
   959→$s \\cap s = s$ — intersection is **idempotent**.
   960→
   961→The proof introduced two key notations:
   962→- **`.1` and `.2`**: extract components from a conjunction hypothesis.
   963→  If `h : P ∧ Q`, then `h.1 : P` and `h.2 : Q`.
   964→- **`⟨a, b⟩`**: build a conjunction in one line. `⟨hs, hs⟩` builds
   965→  `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s` by using the same proof twice.
   966→
   967→You already know `constructor` splits a conjunction goal into two
   968→subgoals. The anonymous constructor `⟨a, b⟩` is a one-line alternative
   969→when both components are short.
   970→
   971→This was the simplest `ext` identity: the logic reduced to
   972→$P \\land P \\iff P$. Next you'll see identities where the logic is
   973→more interesting.
   974→"

   979→=== L10_EmptySetIdentity.lean ===
   980→import Game.Metadata
   981→
   982→World "FinsetOperations"
   983→Level 10
   984→
   985→Title "Empty Set Identity"
   986→
   987→Introduction "
   988→# Union with the Empty Set
   989→
   990→Another set identity using `ext`: prove that
   991→$s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.
   992→
   993→Unioning with the empty set adds nothing — every element of `s ∪ ∅`
   994→is either in `s` (which contributes) or in `∅` (which contributes
   995→nothing, since `∅` has no elements).
   996→
   997→After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes:
   998→$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in \\varnothing \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
   999→
  1000→**Forward**: If `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ ∅`, case-split. In the `x ∈ s` case,
  1001→done. In the `x ∈ ∅` case, this is impossible — recall from Finset
  1002→Basics that `Finset.notMem_empty x` proves `x ∉ ∅`.
  1003→
  1004→**Backward**: If `x ∈ s`, choose `left`.
  1005→
  1006→**Recall**: `exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)` closes a goal
  1007→when you have `he : x ∈ ∅`. The function `absurd` (from MeetFin) derives
  1008→anything from a proof and its negation: since `he` says `x ∈ ∅` and
  1009→`Finset.notMem_empty x` says `x ∉ ∅`, the contradiction closes the goal.
  1010→
  1011→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.
  1012→"
  1013→
  1014→/-- Union with the empty set is the identity: s ∪ ∅ = s. -/
  1015→Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ ∅ = s := by
  1016→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1017→  membership biconditional."
  1018→  ext x
  1019→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to unfold the union."
  1020→  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  1021→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ ∅ ↔ x ∈ s`.
  1022→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1023→  constructor
  1024→  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
  1025→    intro h
  1026→    cases h with
  1027→    | inl hs =>
  1028→      Hint (hidden := true) "`exact hs`."
  1029→      exact hs
  1030→    | inr he =>
  1031→      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `he : x ∈ ∅`, but the empty set
  1032→      has no elements. Use `exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)`."
  1033→      exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)
  1034→  · Hint "If `x ∈ s`, choose the left side:
  1035→    `intro hs; left; exact hs`."
  1036→    intro hs
  1037→    left
  1038→    exact hs
  1039→
  1040→Conclusion "
  1041→You've proved the **union identity law**: $s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.
  1042→
  1043→The key observation: the `x ∈ ∅` case is *vacuously true* — it can
  1044→never actually occur, so you can dismiss it with `absurd`.
  1045→
  1046→The dual identity $s \\cap \\varnothing = \\varnothing$ also holds.
  1047→After `ext x; rw [Finset.mem_inter]`, the goal becomes
  1048→`x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ ∅ ↔ x ∈ ∅`. The forward direction extracts `x ∈ ∅`
  1049→via `.2`. The backward direction is vacuously true (same `absurd` move).
  1050→
  1051→These identity laws are the 'base cases' of set algebra — they'll
  1052→reappear when you study cardinality: `card(s ∪ ∅) = card(s)` and
  1053→`card(s ∩ ∅) = 0`.
  1054→"

  1059→=== L11_DisjointFinsets.lean ===
  1060→import Game.Metadata
  1061→
  1062→World "FinsetOperations"
  1063→Level 11
  1064→
  1065→Title "Disjoint Finsets"
  1066→
  1067→Introduction "
  1068→# When Two Sets Share Nothing
  1069→
  1070→Two sets are **disjoint** when they share no elements — their
  1071→intersection is empty: $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$.
  1072→
  1073→This concept is fundamental to counting: when you compute
  1074→$|s \\cup t|$, the formula simplifies from $|s| + |t| - |s \\cap t|$
  1075→to just $|s| + |t|$ when $s$ and $t$ are disjoint.
  1076→
  1077→**Your task**: Given a hypothesis that no element belongs to both
  1078→$s$ and $t$, prove $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$.
  1079→
  1080→The proof uses the same `ext` recipe: reduce the equality to
  1081→element-wise reasoning, then use the hypothesis to close each case.
  1082→"
  1083→
  1084→/-- If no element belongs to both sets, their intersection is empty. -/
  1085→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (h : ∀ x, x ∈ s → x ∉ t) : s ∩ t = ∅ := by
  1086→  Hint "Reduce the equality to element-wise reasoning with `ext x`,
  1087→  then `constructor`."
  1088→  ext x
  1089→  constructor
  1090→  · Hint "If `x ∈ s ∩ t`, unfold the intersection to get `x ∈ s` and
  1091→    `x ∈ t`. The hypothesis `h` says these are contradictory."
  1092→    intro hx
  1093→    rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx
  1094→    Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hx.1 : x ∈ s` and `hx.2 : x ∈ t`.
  1095→    Apply `h x hx.1` to get `x ∉ t`, then
  1096→    `exact absurd hx.2 (h x hx.1)`."
  1097→    exact absurd hx.2 (h x hx.1)
  1098→  · Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hx : x ∈ ∅`, which is impossible.
  1099→    Use `exact absurd hx (Finset.notMem_empty x)`."
  1100→    intro hx
  1101→    exact absurd hx (Finset.notMem_empty x)
  1102→
  1103→Conclusion "
  1104→Two sets are **disjoint** when $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$ — they share
  1105→no elements.
  1106→
  1107→The proof pattern: `ext` reduces the equality to membership, the forward
  1108→direction contradicts the shared-element assumption, and the backward
  1109→direction is vacuously true (nothing belongs to $\\varnothing$).
  1110→
  1111→This concept will become essential when you study cardinality:
  1112→**disjoint union** ($s \\cap t = \\varnothing$) means
  1113→$|s \\cup t| = |s| + |t|$ with no overlap to subtract.
  1114→"
  1115→

  1119→=== L12_Idempotency.lean ===
  1120→import Game.Metadata
  1121→
  1122→World "FinsetOperations"
  1123→Level 12
  1124→
  1125→Title "Idempotency"
  1126→
  1127→Introduction "
  1128→# Union is Idempotent
  1129→
  1130→Here's the simplest non-trivial set identity: $s \\cup s = s$.
  1131→Unioning a set with itself adds nothing.
  1132→
  1133→After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes:
  1134→$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
  1135→
  1136→**Forward**: If `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ s`, case-split. Both cases give `x ∈ s`.
  1137→
  1138→**Backward**: If `x ∈ s`, choose `left` (or `right` — both work).
  1139→
  1140→This is structurally simpler than commutativity: instead of *swapping*
  1141→the components, both branches produce the *same* result.
  1142→
  1143→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup s = s$.
  1144→"
  1145→
  1146→/-- Union is idempotent: s ∪ s = s. -/
  1147→Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ s = s := by
  1148→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1149→  membership biconditional."
  1150→  ext x
  1151→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to unfold the union."
  1152→  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  1153→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ s`.
  1154→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1155→  constructor
  1156→  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
  1157→    intro h
  1158→    Hint (hidden := true) "Both cases give `x ∈ s`:
  1159→    `| inl hs => exact hs` and `| inr hs => exact hs`."
  1160→    cases h with
  1161→    | inl hs => exact hs
  1162→    | inr hs => exact hs
  1163→  · Hint "If `x ∈ s`, choose either side:
  1164→    `intro hs; left; exact hs`."
  1165→    intro hs
  1166→    left
  1167→    exact hs
  1168→
  1169→Conclusion "
  1170→$s \\cup s = s$ — union is **idempotent** (applying it twice gives
  1171→the same result).
  1172→
  1173→The proof was even simpler than commutativity: both cases of the
  1174→forward direction produce the same output. This is the set-level
  1175→version of $P \\lor P \\iff P$.
  1176→
  1177→The **dual** ($s \\cap s = s$) was Level 9 — you already proved it!
  1178→Union idempotency uses `cases` + both branches identical; intersection
  1179→idempotency uses `.1` projection + `⟨hs, hs⟩` duplication.
  1180→
  1181→Next: commutativity — where you swap the two sides instead of
  1182→collapsing them.
  1183→"
  1184→

  1188→=== L13_IntersectionCommutativity.lean ===
  1189→import Game.Metadata
  1190→
  1191→World "FinsetOperations"
  1192→Level 13
  1193→
  1194→Title "Intersection Commutativity"
  1195→
  1196→Introduction "
  1197→# Proving a Set Identity with ext
  1198→
  1199→Now you'll prove **intersection commutativity**: $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$.
  1200→The proof uses `.1`/`.2` and `⟨,⟩` from Level 9 to swap the conjunction
  1201→components in both directions.
  1202→
  1203→**A note on notation**: Finset `∪` and `∩` are actually lattice
  1204→operations `⊔` and `⊓` under the hood. You may occasionally see `⊔` or
  1205→`⊓` in the infoview. This is just an alternative notation — the
  1206→membership lemmas (`mem_union`, `mem_inter`) work the same way.
  1207→
  1208→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$.
  1209→"
  1210→
  1211→/-- Intersection is commutative. -/
  1212→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ t = t ∩ s := by
  1213→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1214→  membership biconditional."
  1215→  ext x
  1216→  Hint "Rewrite both intersection memberships:
  1217→  `rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]`.
  1218→  The first rewrite handles `x ∈ s ∩ t`, the second handles `x ∈ t ∩ s`."
  1219→  rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]
  1220→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`. Use
  1221→  `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1222→  constructor
  1223→  · Hint "Given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`, you need `x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`.
  1224→    The components are `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : x ∈ t`. Swap them:
  1225→    `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`."
  1226→    intro h
  1227→    exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩
  1228→  · Hint "Same idea in reverse: `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`."
  1229→    intro h
  1230→    exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩
  1231→
  1232→Conclusion "
  1233→You just proved an abstract set identity! The proof structure:
  1234→
  1235→1. `ext x` — convert equality to element-wise `↔`
  1236→2. `rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]` — convert to logic
  1237→3. `constructor` — split the `↔`
  1238→4. Each direction: swap the conjunction components
  1239→
  1240→The new moves:
  1241→- **`.1` and `.2`**: extract components from a conjunction hypothesis.
  1242→  If `h : P ∧ Q`, then `h.1 : P` and `h.2 : Q`.
  1243→- **`⟨a, b⟩`**: build a conjunction in one line. `⟨h.2, h.1⟩` builds
  1244→  a proof of `Q ∧ P` from `h : P ∧ Q`.
  1245→
  1246→This is the power of `ext`: once you reduce a set equation to logic,
  1247→you can use purely logical reasoning. Union gives `∨`, intersection
  1248→gives `∧`, and the proof becomes a logic exercise.
  1249→"
  1250→

  1254→=== L14_UnionCommutativity.lean ===
  1255→import Game.Metadata
  1256→
  1257→World "FinsetOperations"
  1258→Level 14
  1259→
  1260→Title "Union Commutativity"
  1261→
  1262→Introduction "
  1263→# ext with Disjunction
  1264→
  1265→In Level 12 you proved $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$ — intersection commutativity.
  1266→The proof used `ext`, then swapped conjunction components with `.1`/`.2`
  1267→and `⟨h.2, h.1⟩`.
  1268→
  1269→Now try the dual: **union commutativity**, $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$.
  1270→
  1271→After `ext x` and rewriting with `mem_union`, the goal becomes:
  1272→$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in t \\lor x \\in s$$
  1273→
  1274→This time you're swapping *disjunction* components, not conjunction.
  1275→You can't use `.1`/`.2` on a disjunction — instead, you need `cases`
  1276→to split it, then `left`/`right` to rebuild in the other order.
  1277→
  1278→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$.
  1279→"
  1280→
  1281→/-- Union is commutative. -/
  1282→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ t = t ∪ s := by
  1283→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1284→  membership biconditional."
  1285→  ext x
  1286→  Hint "Rewrite both union memberships:
  1287→  `rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]`."
  1288→  rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]
  1289→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`. Use
  1290→  `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1291→  constructor
  1292→  · Hint "Given a disjunction, you need to swap the sides.
  1293→    Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split on which side holds."
  1294→    intro h
  1295→    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h with` gives `| inl hs => ...`
  1296→    and `| inr ht => ...`. In the first case, choose `right`;
  1297→    in the second, choose `left`."
  1298→    cases h with
  1299→    | inl hs =>
  1300→      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hs : x ∈ s`. The goal needs
  1301→      `x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`, so choose `right`: `right; exact hs`."
  1302→      right; exact hs
  1303→    | inr ht =>
  1304→      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; exact ht`."
  1305→      left; exact ht
  1306→  · Hint "Same pattern in reverse: `intro h; cases h with`."
  1307→    intro h
  1308→    Hint (hidden := true) "Swap the sides again: `inl` case gets
  1309→    `right`, `inr` case gets `left`."
  1310→    cases h with
  1311→    | inl ht =>
  1312→      right; exact ht
  1313→    | inr hs =>
  1314→      left; exact hs
  1315→
  1316→Conclusion "
  1317→You've now proved commutativity for both intersection (∧-swapping)
  1318→and union (∨-swapping). Compare the two patterns:
  1319→
  1320→**Intersection commutativity** (Level 12):
  1321→- After ext + rewrite: `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`
  1322→- Swap conjuncts: `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`
  1323→
  1324→**Union commutativity** (this level):
  1325→- After ext + rewrite: `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`
  1326→- Swap disjuncts: `intro h; cases h with | inl => right | inr => left`
  1327→
  1328→The key difference: conjunctions let you project (`.1`/`.2`), but
  1329→disjunctions require case-splitting (`cases`) and rebuilding
  1330→(`left`/`right`). Both patterns appear in the boss.
  1331→"
  1332→

  1336→=== L15_UnionAssociativity.lean ===
  1337→import Game.Metadata
  1338→
  1339→World "FinsetOperations"
  1340→Level 15
  1341→
  1342→Title "Union Associativity"
  1343→
  1344→Introduction "
  1345→# Associativity: Grouping Doesn't Matter
  1346→
  1347→You've proved commutativity (order doesn't matter) and idempotency
  1348→(repeating doesn't matter). Now prove **associativity**: grouping
  1349→doesn't matter.
  1350→
  1351→$$(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$$
  1352→
  1353→After `ext x` and unfolding all unions with `rw [Finset.mem_union]`
  1354→(applied multiple times), the goal becomes:
  1355→
  1356→$$(x \\in s \\lor x \\in t) \\lor x \\in u \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s \\lor (x \\in t \\lor x \\in u)$$
  1357→
  1358→This is **associativity of disjunction**: $(P \\lor Q) \\lor R \\iff P \\lor (Q \\lor R)$.
  1359→
  1360→Both directions require nested `cases` to split the outer disjunction,
  1361→then the inner one. In each leaf case, rebuild with the correct nesting
  1362→of `left`/`right`.
  1363→
  1364→**Your task**: Prove that $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$.
  1365→"
  1366→
  1367→/-- Union is associative. -/
  1368→Statement (s t u : Finset ℕ) : (s ∪ t) ∪ u = s ∪ (t ∪ u) := by
  1369→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1370→  membership biconditional."
  1371→  ext x
  1372→  Hint "Unfold all unions. Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` repeatedly
  1373→  (four times total) to unfold each `∪` into `∨`.
  1374→
  1375→  Alternatively, use `rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union,
  1376→  Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]` in one step."
  1377→  rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]
  1378→  Hint "The goal is `(x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u ↔ x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`.
  1379→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1380→  constructor
  1381→  -- Forward: (x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u → x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)
  1382→  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the outer disjunction."
  1383→    intro h
  1384→    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h with` gives `| inl hst => ...` (where
  1385→    `hst : x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t`) and `| inr hu => ...` (where `hu : x ∈ u`).
  1386→    In the `inl` case, split `hst` with another `cases`.
  1387→    In the `inr` case, `right; right; exact hu`."
  1388→    cases h with
  1389→    | inl hst =>
  1390→      Hint (hidden := true) "Split `hst : x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t` with `cases hst with`.
  1391→      If `x ∈ s`: `left; exact hs`.
  1392→      If `x ∈ t`: `right; left; exact ht`."
  1393→      cases hst with
  1394→      | inl hs => left; exact hs
  1395→      | inr ht => right; left; exact ht
  1396→    | inr hu => right; right; exact hu
  1397→  -- Backward: x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → (x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u
  1398→  · Hint "Same pattern: `intro h; cases h with`."
  1399→    intro h
  1400→    Hint (hidden := true) "In the `inl` case (`hs : x ∈ s`): `left; left; exact hs`.
  1401→    In the `inr` case, split `htu : x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u` with another `cases`.
  1402→    If `x ∈ t`: `left; right; exact ht`.
  1403→    If `x ∈ u`: `right; exact hu`."
  1404→    cases h with
  1405→    | inl hs => left; left; exact hs
  1406→    | inr htu =>
  1407→      cases htu with
  1408→      | inl ht => left; right; exact ht
  1409→      | inr hu => right; exact hu
  1410→
  1411→Conclusion "
  1412→Union is **associative**: $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$.
  1413→
  1414→The proof required **nested case-splitting** — the outer `cases` splits
  1415→the top-level `∨`, and an inner `cases` splits the nested `∨`. Each leaf
  1416→case rebuilds the goal with the correct nesting of `left`/`right`.
  1417→
  1418→With commutativity (Level 12-13), idempotency (Level 9, 11), identity
  1419→(Level 10), and now associativity, you've covered the fundamental
  1420→algebraic laws for union. These same laws hold for intersection (with
  1421→`∧` in place of `∨`) — the **dual** identity
  1422→$(s \\cap t) \\cap u = s \\cap (t \\cap u)$ has a structurally identical
  1423→proof using `.1`/`.2` and `⟨,⟩` instead of `cases` and `left`/`right`.
  1424→
  1425→Together, these laws make `Finset` with `∪` and `∩` a **lattice** — a
  1426→structure you'll see more of in the next level.
  1427→"
  1428→

  1432→=== L16_LatticeNotation.lean ===
  1433→import Game.Metadata
  1434→
  1435→World "FinsetOperations"
  1436→Level 16
  1437→
  1438→Title "Lattice Notation"
  1439→
  1440→Introduction "
  1441→# Lattice Notation: ⊔ and ⊓
  1442→
  1443→In Lean, finset union and intersection are implemented through the
  1444→**lattice** structure. This means you'll sometimes see alternative
  1445→notation in the infoview:
  1446→
  1447→- `s ⊔ t` is the same as `s ∪ t` (lattice 'supremum' = union)
  1448→- `s ⊓ t` is the same as `s ∩ t` (lattice 'infimum' = intersection)
  1449→
  1450→These are **exactly the same operations** — just different notation.
  1451→
  1452→**The problem**: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` matches `∪` but not `⊔`.
  1453→When lattice notation appears, you need to convert it first using
  1454→`show` (for the goal) or `change` (for hypotheses):
  1455→
  1456→- `show x ∈ s ∪ t` converts `x ∈ s ⊔ t` to `x ∈ s ∪ t` in the goal
  1457→- `change x ∈ s ∩ t at h` converts `x ∈ s ⊓ t` to `x ∈ s ∩ t` in `h`
  1458→
  1459→After converting, all the familiar membership lemmas work normally.
  1460→
  1461→**Your task**: Given `x ∈ s ⊓ t` (intersection via lattice notation),
  1462→prove `x ∈ s ⊔ t` (union via lattice notation).
  1463→"
  1464→
  1465→/-- From intersection to union via lattice notation. -/
  1466→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (x : ℕ) (h : x ∈ s ⊓ t) : x ∈ s ⊔ t := by
  1467→  Hint "The goal uses `⊔` (lattice supremum = union). Convert it
  1468→  to familiar notation with `show x ∈ s ∪ t`."
  1469→  show x ∈ s ∪ t
  1470→  Hint "The hypothesis uses `⊓` (lattice infimum = intersection).
  1471→  Convert it with `change x ∈ s ∩ t at h`."
  1472→  change x ∈ s ∩ t at h
  1473→  Hint "Now both goal and hypothesis use familiar notation.
  1474→  Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`."
  1475→  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h
  1476→  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  1477→  Hint (hidden := true) "Choose `left` and use `exact h.1`."
  1478→  left
  1479→  exact h.1
  1480→
  1481→Conclusion "
  1482→The key insight: `⊔` = `∪` and `⊓` = `∩` for finsets, but `rw`
  1483→can't see through the notation difference. Use `show` (goal) or
  1484→`change` (hypothesis) to convert lattice notation to finset notation
  1485→before rewriting.
  1486→
  1487→The workflow when you encounter lattice notation:
  1488→1. **Recognize** `⊔` as union and `⊓` as intersection
  1489→2. **Convert** with `show`/`change` to familiar notation
  1490→3. **Proceed** with `rw [Finset.mem_union]` etc. as usual
  1491→
  1492→You may also encounter `≤` in place of `⊆` (lattice order = subset).
  1493→The same `show`/`change` technique handles it.
  1494→
  1495→In the next level, you'll learn `simp only` — a tool for unfolding
  1496→multiple membership layers at once.
  1497→"
  1498→
  1499→NewTactic «show» «change»

=== L09_IntersectionIdempotency.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 9

Title "Intersection Idempotency"

Introduction "
# Your First ext Identity: s ∩ s = s

The simplest set identity you can prove with `ext`: intersecting a
set with itself gives back the same set.

After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_inter]`, the goal becomes:
$$x \\in s \\land x \\in s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$

**New notation**: `.1` and `.2` on conjunction hypotheses.

When you have `h : P ∧ Q`, you can extract:
- `h.1` gives the first component (`P`)
- `h.2` gives the second component (`Q`)

And to *build* a conjunction, use anonymous constructors:
`⟨proof_of_P, proof_of_Q⟩` is shorthand for `And.intro proof_of_P proof_of_Q`.

**Forward**: given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, project with `h.1`.

**Backward**: given `hs : x ∈ s`, duplicate with `⟨hs, hs⟩`.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cap s = s$.
"

/-- Intersection is idempotent: s ∩ s = s. -/
Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ s = s := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to unfold the intersection."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ s`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  · Hint "Given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s`.
    Use `intro h` and then `exact h.1` to project the first component."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h.1` — the `.1` projection extracts
    the first component of the conjunction."
    exact h.1
  · Hint "Given `hs : x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`.
    Use `intro hs` and then `exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` to build the conjunction."
    intro hs
    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` — the anonymous constructor
    builds the conjunction by duplicating the same proof."
    exact ⟨hs, hs⟩

Conclusion "
$s \\cap s = s$ — intersection is **idempotent**.

The proof introduced two key notations:
- **`.1` and `.2`**: extract components from a conjunction hypothesis.
  If `h : P ∧ Q`, then `h.1 : P` and `h.2 : Q`.
- **`⟨a, b⟩`**: build a conjunction in one line. `⟨hs, hs⟩` builds
  `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s` by using the same proof twice.

You already know `constructor` splits a conjunction goal into two
subgoals. The anonymous constructor `⟨a, b⟩` is a one-line alternative
when both components are short.

This was the simplest `ext` identity: the logic reduced to
$P \\land P \\iff P$. Next you'll see identities where the logic is
more interesting.
"

=== L10_EmptySetIdentity.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 10

Title "Empty Set Identity"

Introduction "
# Union with the Empty Set

Another set identity using `ext`: prove that
$s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.

Unioning with the empty set adds nothing — every element of `s ∪ ∅`
is either in `s` (which contributes) or in `∅` (which contributes
nothing, since `∅` has no elements).

After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes:
$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in \\varnothing \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$

**Forward**: If `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ ∅`, case-split. In the `x ∈ s` case,
done. In the `x ∈ ∅` case, this is impossible — recall from Finset
Basics that `Finset.notMem_empty x` proves `x ∉ ∅`.

**Backward**: If `x ∈ s`, choose `left`.

**Recall**: `exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)` closes a goal
when you have `he : x ∈ ∅`. The function `absurd` (from MeetFin) derives
anything from a proof and its negation: since `he` says `x ∈ ∅` and
`Finset.notMem_empty x` says `x ∉ ∅`, the contradiction closes the goal.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.
"

/-- Union with the empty set is the identity: s ∪ ∅ = s. -/
Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ ∅ = s := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to unfold the union."
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ ∅ ↔ x ∈ s`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
    intro h
    cases h with
    | inl hs =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`exact hs`."
      exact hs
    | inr he =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `he : x ∈ ∅`, but the empty set
      has no elements. Use `exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)`."
      exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)
  · Hint "If `x ∈ s`, choose the left side:
    `intro hs; left; exact hs`."
    intro hs
    left
    exact hs

Conclusion "
You've proved the **union identity law**: $s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.

The key observation: the `x ∈ ∅` case is *vacuously true* — it can
never actually occur, so you can dismiss it with `absurd`.

The dual identity $s \\cap \\varnothing = \\varnothing$ also holds.
After `ext x; rw [Finset.mem_inter]`, the goal becomes
`x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ ∅ ↔ x ∈ ∅`. The forward direction extracts `x ∈ ∅`
via `.2`. The backward direction is vacuously true (same `absurd` move).

These identity laws are the 'base cases' of set algebra — they'll
reappear when you study cardinality: `card(s ∪ ∅) = card(s)` and
`card(s ∩ ∅) = 0`.
"

=== L11_DisjointFinsets.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 11

Title "Disjoint Finsets"

Introduction "
# When Two Sets Share Nothing

Two sets are **disjoint** when they share no elements — their
intersection is empty: $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$.

This concept is fundamental to counting: when you compute
$|s \\cup t|$, the formula simplifies from $|s| + |t| - |s \\cap t|$
to just $|s| + |t|$ when $s$ and $t$ are disjoint.

**Your task**: Given a hypothesis that no element belongs to both
$s$ and $t$, prove $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$.

The proof uses the same `ext` recipe: reduce the equality to
element-wise reasoning, then use the hypothesis to close each case.
"

/-- If no element belongs to both sets, their intersection is empty. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (h : ∀ x, x ∈ s → x ∉ t) : s ∩ t = ∅ := by
  Hint "Reduce the equality to element-wise reasoning with `ext x`,
  then `constructor`."
  ext x
  constructor
  · Hint "If `x ∈ s ∩ t`, unfold the intersection to get `x ∈ s` and
    `x ∈ t`. The hypothesis `h` says these are contradictory."
    intro hx
    rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx
    Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hx.1 : x ∈ s` and `hx.2 : x ∈ t`.
    Apply `h x hx.1` to get `x ∉ t`, then
    `exact absurd hx.2 (h x hx.1)`."
    exact absurd hx.2 (h x hx.1)
  · Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hx : x ∈ ∅`, which is impossible.
    Use `exact absurd hx (Finset.notMem_empty x)`."
    intro hx
    exact absurd hx (Finset.notMem_empty x)

Conclusion "
Two sets are **disjoint** when $s \\cap t = \\varnothing$ — they share
no elements.

The proof pattern: `ext` reduces the equality to membership, the forward
direction contradicts the shared-element assumption, and the backward
direction is vacuously true (nothing belongs to $\\varnothing$).

This concept will become essential when you study cardinality:
**disjoint union** ($s \\cap t = \\varnothing$) means
$|s \\cup t| = |s| + |t|$ with no overlap to subtract.
"

=== L12_Idempotency.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 12

Title "Idempotency"

Introduction "
# Union is Idempotent

Here's the simplest non-trivial set identity: $s \\cup s = s$.
Unioning a set with itself adds nothing.

After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes:
$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$

**Forward**: If `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ s`, case-split. Both cases give `x ∈ s`.

**Backward**: If `x ∈ s`, choose `left` (or `right` — both work).

This is structurally simpler than commutativity: instead of *swapping*
the components, both branches produce the *same* result.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup s = s$.
"

/-- Union is idempotent: s ∪ s = s. -/
Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ s = s := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to unfold the union."
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ s`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Both cases give `x ∈ s`:
    `| inl hs => exact hs` and `| inr hs => exact hs`."
    cases h with
    | inl hs => exact hs
    | inr hs => exact hs
  · Hint "If `x ∈ s`, choose either side:
    `intro hs; left; exact hs`."
    intro hs
    left
    exact hs

Conclusion "
$s \\cup s = s$ — union is **idempotent** (applying it twice gives
the same result).

The proof was even simpler than commutativity: both cases of the
forward direction produce the same output. This is the set-level
version of $P \\lor P \\iff P$.

The **dual** ($s \\cap s = s$) was Level 9 — you already proved it!
Union idempotency uses `cases` + both branches identical; intersection
idempotency uses `.1` projection + `⟨hs, hs⟩` duplication.

Next: commutativity — where you swap the two sides instead of
collapsing them.
"

=== L13_IntersectionCommutativity.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 13

Title "Intersection Commutativity"

Introduction "
# Proving a Set Identity with ext

Now you'll prove **intersection commutativity**: $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$.
The proof uses `.1`/`.2` and `⟨,⟩` from Level 9 to swap the conjunction
components in both directions.

**A note on notation**: Finset `∪` and `∩` are actually lattice
operations `⊔` and `⊓` under the hood. You may occasionally see `⊔` or
`⊓` in the infoview. This is just an alternative notation — the
membership lemmas (`mem_union`, `mem_inter`) work the same way.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$.
"

/-- Intersection is commutative. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ t = t ∩ s := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Rewrite both intersection memberships:
  `rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]`.
  The first rewrite handles `x ∈ s ∩ t`, the second handles `x ∈ t ∩ s`."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`. Use
  `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  · Hint "Given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`, you need `x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`.
    The components are `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : x ∈ t`. Swap them:
    `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`."
    intro h
    exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩
  · Hint "Same idea in reverse: `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`."
    intro h
    exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩

Conclusion "
You just proved an abstract set identity! The proof structure:

1. `ext x` — convert equality to element-wise `↔`
2. `rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_inter]` — convert to logic
3. `constructor` — split the `↔`
4. Each direction: swap the conjunction components

The new moves:
- **`.1` and `.2`**: extract components from a conjunction hypothesis.
  If `h : P ∧ Q`, then `h.1 : P` and `h.2 : Q`.
- **`⟨a, b⟩`**: build a conjunction in one line. `⟨h.2, h.1⟩` builds
  a proof of `Q ∧ P` from `h : P ∧ Q`.

This is the power of `ext`: once you reduce a set equation to logic,
you can use purely logical reasoning. Union gives `∨`, intersection
gives `∧`, and the proof becomes a logic exercise.
"

=== L14_UnionCommutativity.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 14

Title "Union Commutativity"

Introduction "
# ext with Disjunction

In Level 12 you proved $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$ — intersection commutativity.
The proof used `ext`, then swapped conjunction components with `.1`/`.2`
and `⟨h.2, h.1⟩`.

Now try the dual: **union commutativity**, $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$.

After `ext x` and rewriting with `mem_union`, the goal becomes:
$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in t \\lor x \\in s$$

This time you're swapping *disjunction* components, not conjunction.
You can't use `.1`/`.2` on a disjunction — instead, you need `cases`
to split it, then `left`/`right` to rebuild in the other order.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$.
"

/-- Union is commutative. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ t = t ∪ s := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Rewrite both union memberships:
  `rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`. Use
  `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  · Hint "Given a disjunction, you need to swap the sides.
    Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split on which side holds."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h with` gives `| inl hs => ...`
    and `| inr ht => ...`. In the first case, choose `right`;
    in the second, choose `left`."
    cases h with
    | inl hs =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hs : x ∈ s`. The goal needs
      `x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`, so choose `right`: `right; exact hs`."
      right; exact hs
    | inr ht =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; exact ht`."
      left; exact ht
  · Hint "Same pattern in reverse: `intro h; cases h with`."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Swap the sides again: `inl` case gets
    `right`, `inr` case gets `left`."
    cases h with
    | inl ht =>
      right; exact ht
    | inr hs =>
      left; exact hs

Conclusion "
You've now proved commutativity for both intersection (∧-swapping)
and union (∨-swapping). Compare the two patterns:

**Intersection commutativity** (Level 12):
- After ext + rewrite: `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∧ x ∈ s`
- Swap conjuncts: `intro h; exact ⟨h.2, h.1⟩`

**Union commutativity** (this level):
- After ext + rewrite: `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t ↔ x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ s`
- Swap disjuncts: `intro h; cases h with | inl => right | inr => left`

The key difference: conjunctions let you project (`.1`/`.2`), but
disjunctions require case-splitting (`cases`) and rebuilding
(`left`/`right`). Both patterns appear in the boss.
"

=== L15_UnionAssociativity.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 15

Title "Union Associativity"

Introduction "
# Associativity: Grouping Doesn't Matter

You've proved commutativity (order doesn't matter) and idempotency
(repeating doesn't matter). Now prove **associativity**: grouping
doesn't matter.

$$(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$$

After `ext x` and unfolding all unions with `rw [Finset.mem_union]`
(applied multiple times), the goal becomes:

$$(x \\in s \\lor x \\in t) \\lor x \\in u \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s \\lor (x \\in t \\lor x \\in u)$$

This is **associativity of disjunction**: $(P \\lor Q) \\lor R \\iff P \\lor (Q \\lor R)$.

Both directions require nested `cases` to split the outer disjunction,
then the inner one. In each leaf case, rebuild with the correct nesting
of `left`/`right`.

**Your task**: Prove that $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$.
"

/-- Union is associative. -/
Statement (s t u : Finset ℕ) : (s ∪ t) ∪ u = s ∪ (t ∪ u) := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Unfold all unions. Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` repeatedly
  (four times total) to unfold each `∪` into `∨`.

  Alternatively, use `rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union,
  Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]` in one step."
  rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is `(x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u ↔ x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  -- Forward: (x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u → x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)
  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the outer disjunction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h with` gives `| inl hst => ...` (where
    `hst : x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t`) and `| inr hu => ...` (where `hu : x ∈ u`).
    In the `inl` case, split `hst` with another `cases`.
    In the `inr` case, `right; right; exact hu`."
    cases h with
    | inl hst =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Split `hst : x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t` with `cases hst with`.
      If `x ∈ s`: `left; exact hs`.
      If `x ∈ t`: `right; left; exact ht`."
      cases hst with
      | inl hs => left; exact hs
      | inr ht => right; left; exact ht
    | inr hu => right; right; exact hu
  -- Backward: x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → (x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ∨ x ∈ u
  · Hint "Same pattern: `intro h; cases h with`."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "In the `inl` case (`hs : x ∈ s`): `left; left; exact hs`.
    In the `inr` case, split `htu : x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u` with another `cases`.
    If `x ∈ t`: `left; right; exact ht`.
    If `x ∈ u`: `right; exact hu`."
    cases h with
    | inl hs => left; left; exact hs
    | inr htu =>
      cases htu with
      | inl ht => left; right; exact ht
      | inr hu => right; exact hu

Conclusion "
Union is **associative**: $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$.

The proof required **nested case-splitting** — the outer `cases` splits
the top-level `∨`, and an inner `cases` splits the nested `∨`. Each leaf
case rebuilds the goal with the correct nesting of `left`/`right`.

With commutativity (Level 12-13), idempotency (Level 9, 11), identity
(Level 10), and now associativity, you've covered the fundamental
algebraic laws for union. These same laws hold for intersection (with
`∧` in place of `∨`) — the **dual** identity
$(s \\cap t) \\cap u = s \\cap (t \\cap u)$ has a structurally identical
proof using `.1`/`.2` and `⟨,⟩` instead of `cases` and `left`/`right`.

Together, these laws make `Finset` with `∪` and `∩` a **lattice** — a
structure you'll see more of in the next level.
"

=== L16_LatticeNotation.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 16

Title "Lattice Notation"

Introduction "
# Lattice Notation: ⊔ and ⊓

In Lean, finset union and intersection are implemented through the
**lattice** structure. This means you'll sometimes see alternative
notation in the infoview:

- `s ⊔ t` is the same as `s ∪ t` (lattice 'supremum' = union)
- `s ⊓ t` is the same as `s ∩ t` (lattice 'infimum' = intersection)

These are **exactly the same operations** — just different notation.

**The problem**: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` matches `∪` but not `⊔`.
When lattice notation appears, you need to convert it first using
`show` (for the goal) or `change` (for hypotheses):

- `show x ∈ s ∪ t` converts `x ∈ s ⊔ t` to `x ∈ s ∪ t` in the goal
- `change x ∈ s ∩ t at h` converts `x ∈ s ⊓ t` to `x ∈ s ∩ t` in `h`

After converting, all the familiar membership lemmas work normally.

**Your task**: Given `x ∈ s ⊓ t` (intersection via lattice notation),
prove `x ∈ s ⊔ t` (union via lattice notation).
"

/-- From intersection to union via lattice notation. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (x : ℕ) (h : x ∈ s ⊓ t) : x ∈ s ⊔ t := by
  Hint "The goal uses `⊔` (lattice supremum = union). Convert it
  to familiar notation with `show x ∈ s ∪ t`."
  show x ∈ s ∪ t
  Hint "The hypothesis uses `⊓` (lattice infimum = intersection).
  Convert it with `change x ∈ s ∩ t at h`."
  change x ∈ s ∩ t at h
  Hint "Now both goal and hypothesis use familiar notation.
  Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint (hidden := true) "Choose `left` and use `exact h.1`."
  left
  exact h.1

Conclusion "
The key insight: `⊔` = `∪` and `⊓` = `∩` for finsets, but `rw`
can't see through the notation difference. Use `show` (goal) or
`change` (hypothesis) to convert lattice notation to finset notation
before rewriting.

The workflow when you encounter lattice notation:
1. **Recognize** `⊔` as union and `⊓` as intersection
2. **Convert** with `show`/`change` to familiar notation
3. **Proceed** with `rw [Finset.mem_union]` etc. as usual

You may also encounter `≤` in place of `⊆` (lattice order = subset).
The same `show`/`change` technique handles it.

In the next level, you'll learn `simp only` — a tool for unfolding
multiple membership layers at once.
"

NewTactic «show» «change»

  1504→=== L17_TargetedSimplification.lean ===
  1505→import Game.Metadata
  1506→
  1507→World "FinsetOperations"
  1508→Level 17
  1509→
  1510→Title "Targeted Simplification"
  1511→
  1512→Introduction "
  1513→# simp only: Targeted Rewriting
  1514→
  1515→You've been unfolding membership one `rw` at a time:
  1516→```
  1517→rw [Finset.mem_union] at h
  1518→rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
  1519→rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
  1520→rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h
  1521→```
  1522→
  1523→This works but it's tedious. Lean has a better tool: `simp only`.
  1524→
  1525→`simp only [lemma₁, lemma₂, ...]` applies the listed lemmas as rewrite
  1526→rules **repeatedly** until no more apply. Unlike bare `simp` (which uses
  1527→a large default set of lemmas and can be unpredictable), `simp only`
  1528→uses *only* the lemmas you specify. This makes it:
  1529→- **Targeted**: you control exactly which rewrites happen
  1530→- **Predictable**: no surprise simplifications
  1531→- **Efficient**: replaces multiple `rw` calls with one `simp only`
  1532→
  1533→`simp only` also works on hypotheses: `simp only [...] at h` rewrites
  1534→the hypothesis `h`.
  1535→
  1536→**Note**: Bare `simp` (without `only`) is now available, and it will work
  1537→on some goals. But `simp only` is the skill being taught here — it gives
  1538→you control over exactly which lemmas are applied, which makes proofs
  1539→more readable and maintainable.
  1540→
  1541→**Your task**: An element `x` is in `{{1, 2, 3}} ∪ {{4, 5}}`. Prove
  1542→that `x ∈ Finset.range 6`.
  1543→
  1544→Use `simp only` to unfold the union and concrete memberships in `h`
  1545→in one step, then close with `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`.
  1546→"
  1547→
  1548→/-- If x ∈ {1, 2, 3} ∪ {4, 5}, then x ∈ range 6. -/
  1549→Statement (x : ℕ) (h : x ∈ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ) ∪ ({4, 5} : Finset ℕ)) :
  1550→    x ∈ Finset.range 6 := by
  1551→  Hint "Use `simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`
  1552→  to unfold the union and concrete memberships in `h` all at once."
  1553→  simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
  1554→  Hint "Now `h` is a disjunction of equalities: `x` equals one of
  1555→  `1, 2, 3, 4, 5`. The goal is `x ∈ Finset.range 6`.
  1556→  Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to get `x < 6`, then `omega`
  1557→  closes it from the disjunctive hypothesis."
  1558→  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  1559→  omega
  1560→
  1561→Conclusion "
  1562→`simp only` replaced four or more `rw` calls plus case splitting with
  1563→a single line. After `simp only [mem_union, mem_insert, mem_singleton] at h`,
  1564→the hypothesis `h` becomes a pure disjunction of equalities — all the
  1565→finset structure is gone.
  1566→
  1567→**When to use simp only**:
  1568→- When you need to unfold multiple membership layers at once
  1569→- When the manual `rw` chain would be tedious
  1570→- When you want the result to be pure logic (∨, ∧, ¬)
  1571→
  1572→**Key rule**: Always use `simp only [...]` with an explicit lemma list,
  1573→not bare `simp`. Bare `simp` uses hundreds of default lemmas and can
  1574→produce unpredictable results. `simp only` gives you control.
  1575→
  1576→**Common lemma list for finset membership**:
  1577→```
  1578→simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_sdiff,
  1579→           Finset.mem_filter, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton,
  1580→           Finset.mem_range]
  1581→```
  1582→
  1583→You'll use `simp only` in the upcoming levels to simplify membership
  1584→rewrites before tackling the logic.
  1585→"

  1592→=== L18_DeMorgansLaw.lean ===
  1593→import Game.Metadata
  1594→
  1595→World "FinsetOperations"
  1596→Level 18
  1597→
  1598→Title "De Morgan's Law"
  1599→
  1600→Introduction "
  1601→# De Morgan's Law for Finsets
  1602→
  1603→Remember set difference from Level 3? `s \\\\ t` contains elements in `s`
  1604→but not in `t`, with `mem_sdiff : x ∈ s \\\\ t ↔ x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`.
  1605→
  1606→Now you'll prove a fundamental identity connecting set difference with
  1607→union and intersection — **De Morgan's law for sets**:
  1608→
  1609→$$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$$
  1610→
  1611→In words: removing elements that are in `t` *or* `u` is the same as
  1612→removing elements in `t` AND removing elements in `u`, then taking
  1613→the intersection.
  1614→
  1615→After `ext x` and `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]`,
  1616→the goal becomes pure logic:
  1617→
  1618→$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\lor x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\land (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$
  1619→
  1620→This is the propositional De Morgan's law: $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$,
  1621→wrapped in the common `x ∈ s` hypothesis.
  1622→
  1623→**New move**: To prove a negation like `x ∉ t` (which is `x ∈ t → False`),
  1624→use `intro ht` to assume membership, then derive a contradiction using
  1625→`apply h.2` (where `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`) and `left` or `right`.
  1626→
  1627→**Nested projections**: In the backward direction, you'll encounter a
  1628→nested conjunction `h : (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)`. You can
  1629→chain `.1`/`.2` projections to reach any component:
  1630→- `h.1.1 : x ∈ s` (first component of the first conjunct)
  1631→- `h.1.2 : x ∉ t` (second component of the first conjunct)
  1632→- `h.2.1 : x ∈ s` (first component of the second conjunct)
  1633→- `h.2.2 : x ∉ u` (second component of the second conjunct)
  1634→
  1635→This extends the `.1`/`.2` pattern from Level 12 to nested conjunctions.
  1636→
  1637→**Your task**: Prove De Morgan's law for finsets.
  1638→"
  1639→
  1640→/-- De Morgan's law: s \ (t ∪ u) = (s \ t) ∩ (s \ u). -/
  1641→Statement (s t u : Finset ℕ) : s \ (t ∪ u) = (s \ t) ∩ (s \ u) := by
  1642→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1643→  membership biconditional."
  1644→  ext x
  1645→  Hint "Use `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]`
  1646→  to unfold all operations into pure logic."
  1647→  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]
  1648→  Hint "The goal is now:
  1649→  `x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) ↔ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)`.
  1650→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1651→  constructor
  1652→  -- Forward: x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)
  1653→  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`.
  1654→    The goal is a conjunction of conjunctions — use `constructor` to split."
  1655→    intro h
  1656→    Hint (hidden := true) "After `constructor`, each subgoal is a conjunction.
  1657→    Use `constructor` again. For the `x ∈ s` parts, use `exact h.1`.
  1658→    For the `x ∉ t` part: `intro ht; apply h.2; left; exact ht`.
  1659→    For the `x ∉ u` part: `intro hu; apply h.2; right; exact hu`."
  1660→    constructor
  1661→    · constructor
  1662→      · exact h.1
  1663→      · Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `x ∉ t`, which means `x ∈ t → False`.
  1664→        Use `intro ht` to assume `x ∈ t`. Then `apply h.2` changes the
  1665→        goal to `x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u`. Choose `left; exact ht`."
  1666→        intro ht
  1667→        apply h.2
  1668→        left
  1669→        exact ht
  1670→    · constructor
  1671→      · exact h.1
  1672→      · intro hu
  1673→        apply h.2
  1674→        right
  1675→        exact hu
  1676→  -- Backward: (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u) → x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)
  1677→  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Now `h` is a nested conjunction:
  1678→    `h.1.1 : x ∈ s`, `h.1.2 : x ∉ t`, `h.2.2 : x ∉ u`.
  1679→    Build the goal with `constructor`."
  1680→    intro h
  1681→    Hint "Use `constructor` to split the goal. The first part
  1682→    is `x ∈ s` — use `exact h.1.1`. The second part is
  1683→    `¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)` — you'll handle that next."
  1684→    constructor
  1685→    · exact h.1.1
  1686→    · Hint "The goal is `¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`, which means
  1687→      `(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → False`. Use `intro hor` to assume
  1688→      the disjunction, then `cases hor with` to split into cases."
  1689→      intro hor
  1690→      Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, derive `False`:
  1691→      - `| inl ht =>`: use `exact h.1.2 ht` (since `h.1.2 : x ∉ t`)
  1692→      - `| inr hu =>`: use `exact h.2.2 hu` (since `h.2.2 : x ∉ u`)"
  1693→      cases hor with
  1694→      | inl ht => exact h.1.2 ht
  1695→      | inr hu => exact h.2.2 hu
  1696→
  1697→Conclusion "
  1698→You've proved De Morgan's law for finsets! The set-level identity
  1699→
  1700→$$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$$
  1701→
  1702→is exactly the propositional De Morgan's law
  1703→$\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
  1704→lifted to set membership.
  1705→
  1706→The new technique: **proving a negation by applying a negation hypothesis**.
  1707→When the goal is `False` and you have `h.2 : ¬(P ∨ Q)`, using `apply h.2`
  1708→changes the goal to `P ∨ Q`, which you can then build with `left`/`right`.
  1709→
  1710→This completes the set-operations-are-logic correspondence:
  1711→- Union `∪` ↔ disjunction `∨`
  1712→- Intersection `∩` ↔ conjunction `∧`
  1713→- Set difference `\\\\` ↔ conjunction with negation `∧ ¬`
  1714→- De Morgan connects them all.
  1715→"
  1716→

  1720→=== L19_ProofByContradiction.lean ===
  1721→import Game.Metadata
  1722→
  1723→World "FinsetOperations"
  1724→Level 19
  1725→
  1726→Title "Proof by Contradiction"
  1727→
  1728→Introduction "
  1729→# by_contra: Proof by Contradiction
  1730→
  1731→Sometimes you need to prove something positive (`x ∈ t`) but your
  1732→hypotheses only give you negative information (`x ∉ s \\\\ t`). In
  1733→these cases, **proof by contradiction** is the right move.
  1734→
  1735→**New tactic**: `by_contra hnt` on a goal `P` introduces `hnt : ¬P`
  1736→and changes the goal to `False`. You then derive `False` from
  1737→`hnt` and your other hypotheses.
  1738→
  1739→Here's the setup: you know `x ∈ s` and `x ∉ s \\\\ t`. You need
  1740→`x ∈ t`. The idea: if `x ∉ t`, then `x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t` would give
  1741→`x ∈ s \\\\ t`, contradicting `x ∉ s \\\\ t`.
  1742→
  1743→Note: `simp` is disabled for this and the next two levels to ensure
  1744→you practice `by_contra` and manual proof construction.
  1745→
  1746→**Your task**: From `x ∈ s` and `x ∉ s \\\\ t`, prove `x ∈ t`.
  1747→"
  1748→
  1749→/-- If x is in s but not in s \ t, then x must be in t. -/
  1750→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (x : ℕ) (hs : x ∈ s) (h : x ∉ s \ t) : x ∈ t := by
  1751→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ t`. You can't extract this directly from
  1752→  your hypotheses. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x ∉ t` and derive
  1753→  a contradiction."
  1754→  by_contra hnt
  1755→  Hint "Now `hnt : x ∉ t` (i.e., `¬(x ∈ t)`). You need to derive
  1756→  `False`. You have `h : x ∉ s \\\\ t`. If you can build a proof
  1757→  that `x ∈ s \\\\ t`, applying `h` to it gives `False`.
  1758→  Use `apply h`."
  1759→  apply h
  1760→  Hint "The goal is now `x ∈ s \\\\ t`. Use
  1761→  `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]` to unfold it into a conjunction."
  1762→  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
  1763→  Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`.
  1764→  Use `exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩`."
  1765→  exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩
  1766→
  1767→Conclusion "
  1768→You've used `by_contra` for the first time! The pattern:
  1769→
  1770→1. **`by_contra hnt`** — assume `¬(goal)`, change goal to `False`
  1771→2. **Build a contradiction** — use `hnt` together with other hypotheses
  1772→   to construct something that violates a known fact
  1773→
  1774→In this case: assuming `x ∉ t` let you build `x ∈ s \\\\ t` (from
  1775→`hs` and `hnt`), which contradicted `h : x ∉ s \\\\ t`.
  1776→
  1777→`by_contra` is the tactic version of proof by contradiction — one of
  1778→the most fundamental techniques in mathematics. It's especially
  1779→useful when you need to extract positive information from negative
  1780→hypotheses.
  1781→
  1782→In the next level, you'll use `by_contra` inside a more complex proof:
  1783→the double set difference identity.
  1784→"
  1785→
  1786→NewTactic by_contra
  1787→

  1791→=== L20_ComplementViaSdiff.lean ===
  1792→import Game.Metadata
  1793→
  1794→World "FinsetOperations"
  1795→Level 20
  1796→
  1797→Title "Complement via Set Difference"
  1798→
  1799→Introduction "
  1800→# Double Set Difference and Complement
  1801→
  1802→Here's a useful identity that connects set difference to intersection:
  1803→
  1804→$$s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$$
  1805→
  1806→Read it aloud: 'removing from `s` everything that's in `s` but not in `t`
  1807→leaves exactly the elements of `s` that *are* in `t`.'
  1808→
  1809→This is the **complement principle**: if you think of `s` as a 'universe,'
  1810→then `s \\\\ t` is the 'complement of `t` within `s`.' Taking the complement
  1811→again (`s \\\\ (s \\\\ t)`) gets you back to `s ∩ t` — the part of `t` inside
  1812→your universe.
  1813→
  1814→After `ext x` and rewriting with `Finset.mem_sdiff` (twice) and
  1815→`Finset.mem_inter`, the goal becomes:
  1816→
  1817→$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s \\land x \\in t$$
  1818→
  1819→The backward direction is straightforward: if `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`, then
  1820→`x ∈ s` is immediate, and `¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)` follows because `x ∈ t`
  1821→contradicts `x ∉ t`.
  1822→
  1823→The forward direction needs `by_contra`: you need `x ∈ t`, but your
  1824→hypotheses only tell you `x ∈ s` and `¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)`. Assume
  1825→`x ∉ t` (using `by_contra`), then construct `⟨h.1, hnt⟩ : x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`,
  1826→which contradicts `h.2`.
  1827→
  1828→Note: `simp` is disabled for this level to ensure you practice the
  1829→`by_contra` technique manually.
  1830→
  1831→**Your task**: Prove that $s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$.
  1832→"
  1833→
  1834→/-- Double set difference recovers intersection: s \ (s \ t) = s ∩ t. -/
  1835→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s \ (s \ t) = s ∩ t := by
  1836→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1837→  membership biconditional."
  1838→  ext x
  1839→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter]`
  1840→  to unfold all operations into logic."
  1841→  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter]
  1842→  Hint "The goal is
  1843→  `x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ↔ x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`.
  1844→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1845→  constructor
  1846→  -- Forward: x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) → x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t
  1847→  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and
  1848→    `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)`. The first conjunct of the goal
  1849→    is `h.1`. For the second (`x ∈ t`), use `by_contra`."
  1850→    intro h
  1851→    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `constructor` to split the goal.
  1852→    First part: `exact h.1`. Second part: `by_contra hnt`
  1853→    introduces `hnt : x ∉ t`, then `exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩`."
  1854→    constructor
  1855→    · exact h.1
  1856→    · Hint "The goal is `x ∈ t`. You can't extract this directly
  1857→      from `h`. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x ∉ t` and derive
  1858→      a contradiction."
  1859→      by_contra hnt
  1860→      Hint (hidden := true) "Now `hnt : x ∉ t`. Build the
  1861→      contradicting pair: `exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩`."
  1862→      exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩
  1863→  -- Backward: x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t → x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)
  1864→  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : x ∈ t`.
  1865→    Use `constructor` to split the goal."
  1866→    intro h
  1867→    Hint (hidden := true) "First part: `exact h.1`. Second part:
  1868→    `intro hc; exact hc.2 h.2` — assume `hc : x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`,
  1869→    then `hc.2 : x ∉ t` contradicts `h.2 : x ∈ t`."
  1870→    constructor
  1871→    · exact h.1
  1872→    · intro hc
  1873→      exact hc.2 h.2
  1874→
  1875→Conclusion "
  1876→You've proved the **double set difference identity**:
  1877→$s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$.
  1878→
  1879→The key move was `by_contra`: assume `¬(goal)` and derive `False`.
  1880→Combined with the negated conjunction hypothesis, this extracted
  1881→positive membership information from a double negation.
  1882→
  1883→**The complement perspective**: Think of `s` as a 'universe.' Then:
  1884→- `s \\\\ t` is the 'complement of `t` within `s`' — everything in
  1885→  the universe that isn't in `t`
  1886→- `s \\\\ (s \\\\ t)` is the 'complement of the complement' — which
  1887→  gives back `s ∩ t`
  1888→
  1889→This double-complement pattern is exactly the set-theoretic version
  1890→of double negation: $\\neg\\neg P \\iff P$. It previews the
  1891→**inclusion-exclusion** reasoning you'll use in the Cardinality world,
  1892→where counting elements 'not in the complement' is a key technique.
  1893→"
  1894→

  1898→=== L21_AbsorptionLaw.lean ===
  1899→import Game.Metadata
  1900→
  1901→World "FinsetOperations"
  1902→Level 21
  1903→
  1904→Title "Absorption Law"
  1905→
  1906→Introduction "
  1907→# The Absorption Law
  1908→
  1909→Here's a surprising identity: $s \\cup (s \\cap t) = s$. The intersection
  1910→with `t` adds nothing — `s` already contains everything in `s ∩ t`.
  1911→
  1912→This is the **absorption law**, one of the fundamental lattice identities.
  1913→Unlike commutativity or De Morgan (where both directions have similar
  1914→structure), this proof is **asymmetric**: one direction is trivial,
  1915→the other requires a case split.
  1916→
  1917→After `ext x` and rewriting with `Finset.mem_union` and
  1918→`Finset.mem_inter`, the goal becomes:
  1919→$$x \\in s \\lor (x \\in s \\land x \\in t) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
  1920→
  1921→**Forward** ($\\Rightarrow$): If `x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t)`, case-split.
  1922→In both cases, `x ∈ s` — either directly or via `.1`.
  1923→
  1924→**Backward** ($\\Leftarrow$): If `x ∈ s`, choose `left`.
  1925→
  1926→Note: `simp` is disabled for this level to ensure you practice the
  1927→manual asymmetric proof structure.
  1928→
  1929→**Your task**: Prove the absorption law.
  1930→"
  1931→
  1932→/-- Absorption: s ∪ (s ∩ t) = s. -/
  1933→Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s ∪ (s ∩ t) = s := by
  1934→  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  1935→  membership biconditional."
  1936→  ext x
  1937→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]`
  1938→  to unfold the operations into logic."
  1939→  rw [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]
  1940→  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t) ↔ x ∈ s`.
  1941→  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  1942→  constructor
  1943→  -- Forward: x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t) → x ∈ s
  1944→  · Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
  1945→    intro h
  1946→    Hint (hidden := true) "In both cases, `x ∈ s` follows.
  1947→    For `| inl hs =>`, `exact hs`.
  1948→    For `| inr hst =>`, `exact hst.1`."
  1949→    cases h with
  1950→    | inl hs => exact hs
  1951→    | inr hst => exact hst.1
  1952→  -- Backward: x ∈ s → x ∈ s ∨ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t)
  1953→  · Hint "If `x ∈ s`, choose the left side of the disjunction:
  1954→    `intro hs; left; exact hs`."
  1955→    intro hs
  1956→    left
  1957→    exact hs
  1958→
  1959→Conclusion "
  1960→The absorption law has a beautifully asymmetric proof:
  1961→- **Forward**: both branches of the `∨` give `x ∈ s` — one directly,
  1962→  one via `.1` projection
  1963→- **Backward**: immediate by `left`
  1964→
  1965→This asymmetry is the distinguishing feature: unlike commutativity
  1966→(where both directions are the same swap) or distributivity (where both
  1967→directions have similar case-split structure), absorption has one
  1968→'trivial' direction and one 'case-split' direction.
  1969→
  1970→The absorption law says that intersecting with `t` and then unioning
  1971→back with `s` adds nothing — `s` already 'absorbs' `s ∩ t`.
  1972→
  1973→The **dual absorption law** `s ∩ (s ∪ t) = s` also holds. After
  1974→`ext x; rw [Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes
  1975→`x ∈ s ∧ (x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t) ↔ x ∈ s`. The forward direction uses
  1976→`.1` to project out `x ∈ s` from the conjunction. The backward
  1977→direction uses `⟨hs, Or.inl hs⟩` to build the conjunction. Same
  1978→moves, swapped roles of ∧ and ∨.
  1979→
  1980→Next up: the boss, where you'll prove distributivity of union over
  1981→intersection using all the tools from this world.
  1982→"
  1983→

=== L17_TargetedSimplification.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 17

Title "Targeted Simplification"

Introduction "
# simp only: Targeted Rewriting

You've been unfolding membership one `rw` at a time:
```
rw [Finset.mem_union] at h
rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h
```

This works but it's tedious. Lean has a better tool: `simp only`.

`simp only [lemma₁, lemma₂, ...]` applies the listed lemmas as rewrite
rules **repeatedly** until no more apply. Unlike bare `simp` (which uses
a large default set of lemmas and can be unpredictable), `simp only`
uses *only* the lemmas you specify. This makes it:
- **Targeted**: you control exactly which rewrites happen
- **Predictable**: no surprise simplifications
- **Efficient**: replaces multiple `rw` calls with one `simp only`

`simp only` also works on hypotheses: `simp only [...] at h` rewrites
the hypothesis `h`.

**Note**: Bare `simp` (without `only`) is now available, and it will work
on some goals. But `simp only` is the skill being taught here — it gives
you control over exactly which lemmas are applied, which makes proofs
more readable and maintainable.

**Your task**: An element `x` is in `{{1, 2, 3}} ∪ {{4, 5}}`. Prove
that `x ∈ Finset.range 6`.

Use `simp only` to unfold the union and concrete memberships in `h`
in one step, then close with `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`.
"

/-- If x ∈ {1, 2, 3} ∪ {4, 5}, then x ∈ range 6. -/
Statement (x : ℕ) (h : x ∈ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ) ∪ ({4, 5} : Finset ℕ)) :
    x ∈ Finset.range 6 := by
  Hint "Use `simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`
  to unfold the union and concrete memberships in `h` all at once."
  simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
  Hint "Now `h` is a disjunction of equalities: `x` equals one of
  `1, 2, 3, 4, 5`. The goal is `x ∈ Finset.range 6`.
  Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to get `x < 6`, then `omega`
  closes it from the disjunctive hypothesis."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  omega

Conclusion "
`simp only` replaced four or more `rw` calls plus case splitting with
a single line. After `simp only [mem_union, mem_insert, mem_singleton] at h`,
the hypothesis `h` becomes a pure disjunction of equalities — all the
finset structure is gone.

**When to use simp only**:
- When you need to unfold multiple membership layers at once
- When the manual `rw` chain would be tedious
- When you want the result to be pure logic (∨, ∧, ¬)

**Key rule**: Always use `simp only [...]` with an explicit lemma list,
not bare `simp`. Bare `simp` uses hundreds of default lemmas and can
produce unpredictable results. `simp only` gives you control.

**Common lemma list for finset membership**:
```
simp only [Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_sdiff,
           Finset.mem_filter, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton,
           Finset.mem_range]
```

You'll use `simp only` in the upcoming levels to simplify membership
rewrites before tackling the logic.
"

NewTactic simp

=== L18_DeMorgansLaw.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 18

Title "De Morgan's Law"

Introduction "
# De Morgan's Law for Finsets

Remember set difference from Level 3? `s \\\\ t` contains elements in `s`
but not in `t`, with `mem_sdiff : x ∈ s \\\\ t ↔ x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`.

Now you'll prove a fundamental identity connecting set difference with
union and intersection — **De Morgan's law for sets**:

$$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$$

In words: removing elements that are in `t` *or* `u` is the same as
removing elements in `t` AND removing elements in `u`, then taking
the intersection.

After `ext x` and `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]`,
the goal becomes pure logic:

$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\lor x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\land (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$

This is the propositional De Morgan's law: $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$,
wrapped in the common `x ∈ s` hypothesis.

**New move**: To prove a negation like `x ∉ t` (which is `x ∈ t → False`),
use `intro ht` to assume membership, then derive a contradiction using
`apply h.2` (where `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`) and `left` or `right`.

**Nested projections**: In the backward direction, you'll encounter a
nested conjunction `h : (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)`. You can
chain `.1`/`.2` projections to reach any component:
- `h.1.1 : x ∈ s` (first component of the first conjunct)
- `h.1.2 : x ∉ t` (second component of the first conjunct)
- `h.2.1 : x ∈ s` (first component of the second conjunct)
- `h.2.2 : x ∉ u` (second component of the second conjunct)

This extends the `.1`/`.2` pattern from Level 12 to nested conjunctions.

**Your task**: Prove De Morgan's law for finsets.
"

/-- De Morgan's law: s \ (t ∪ u) = (s \ t) ∩ (s \ u). -/
Statement (s t u : Finset ℕ) : s \ (t ∪ u) = (s \ t) ∩ (s \ u) := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Use `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]`
  to unfold all operations into pure logic."
  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_union, Finset.mem_inter]
  Hint "The goal is now:
  `x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) ↔ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  -- Forward: x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u)
  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`.
    The goal is a conjunction of conjunctions — use `constructor` to split."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `constructor`, each subgoal is a conjunction.
    Use `constructor` again. For the `x ∈ s` parts, use `exact h.1`.
    For the `x ∉ t` part: `intro ht; apply h.2; left; exact ht`.
    For the `x ∉ u` part: `intro hu; apply h.2; right; exact hu`."
    constructor
    · constructor
      · exact h.1
      · Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `x ∉ t`, which means `x ∈ t → False`.
        Use `intro ht` to assume `x ∈ t`. Then `apply h.2` changes the
        goal to `x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u`. Choose `left; exact ht`."
        intro ht
        apply h.2
        left
        exact ht
    · constructor
      · exact h.1
      · intro hu
        apply h.2
        right
        exact hu
  -- Backward: (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ∧ (x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ u) → x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)
  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Now `h` is a nested conjunction:
    `h.1.1 : x ∈ s`, `h.1.2 : x ∉ t`, `h.2.2 : x ∉ u`.
    Build the goal with `constructor`."
    intro h
    Hint "Use `constructor` to split the goal. The first part
    is `x ∈ s` — use `exact h.1.1`. The second part is
    `¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)` — you'll handle that next."
    constructor
    · exact h.1.1
    · Hint "The goal is `¬(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u)`, which means
      `(x ∈ t ∨ x ∈ u) → False`. Use `intro hor` to assume
      the disjunction, then `cases hor with` to split into cases."
      intro hor
      Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, derive `False`:
      - `| inl ht =>`: use `exact h.1.2 ht` (since `h.1.2 : x ∉ t`)
      - `| inr hu =>`: use `exact h.2.2 hu` (since `h.2.2 : x ∉ u`)"
      cases hor with
      | inl ht => exact h.1.2 ht
      | inr hu => exact h.2.2 hu

Conclusion "
You've proved De Morgan's law for finsets! The set-level identity

$$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$$

is exactly the propositional De Morgan's law
$\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
lifted to set membership.

The new technique: **proving a negation by applying a negation hypothesis**.
When the goal is `False` and you have `h.2 : ¬(P ∨ Q)`, using `apply h.2`
changes the goal to `P ∨ Q`, which you can then build with `left`/`right`.

This completes the set-operations-are-logic correspondence:
- Union `∪` ↔ disjunction `∨`
- Intersection `∩` ↔ conjunction `∧`
- Set difference `\\\\` ↔ conjunction with negation `∧ ¬`
- De Morgan connects them all.
"

=== L19_ProofByContradiction.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 19

Title "Proof by Contradiction"

Introduction "
# by_contra: Proof by Contradiction

Sometimes you need to prove something positive (`x ∈ t`) but your
hypotheses only give you negative information (`x ∉ s \\\\ t`). In
these cases, **proof by contradiction** is the right move.

**New tactic**: `by_contra hnt` on a goal `P` introduces `hnt : ¬P`
and changes the goal to `False`. You then derive `False` from
`hnt` and your other hypotheses.

Here's the setup: you know `x ∈ s` and `x ∉ s \\\\ t`. You need
`x ∈ t`. The idea: if `x ∉ t`, then `x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t` would give
`x ∈ s \\\\ t`, contradicting `x ∉ s \\\\ t`.

Note: `simp` is disabled for this and the next two levels to ensure
you practice `by_contra` and manual proof construction.

**Your task**: From `x ∈ s` and `x ∉ s \\\\ t`, prove `x ∈ t`.
"

/-- If x is in s but not in s \ t, then x must be in t. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (x : ℕ) (hs : x ∈ s) (h : x ∉ s \ t) : x ∈ t := by
  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ t`. You can't extract this directly from
  your hypotheses. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x ∉ t` and derive
  a contradiction."
  by_contra hnt
  Hint "Now `hnt : x ∉ t` (i.e., `¬(x ∈ t)`). You need to derive
  `False`. You have `h : x ∉ s \\\\ t`. If you can build a proof
  that `x ∈ s \\\\ t`, applying `h` to it gives `False`.
  Use `apply h`."
  apply h
  Hint "The goal is now `x ∈ s \\\\ t`. Use
  `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]` to unfold it into a conjunction."
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
  Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`.
  Use `exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩`."
  exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩

Conclusion "
You've used `by_contra` for the first time! The pattern:

1. **`by_contra hnt`** — assume `¬(goal)`, change goal to `False`
2. **Build a contradiction** — use `hnt` together with other hypotheses
   to construct something that violates a known fact

In this case: assuming `x ∉ t` let you build `x ∈ s \\\\ t` (from
`hs` and `hnt`), which contradicted `h : x ∉ s \\\\ t`.

`by_contra` is the tactic version of proof by contradiction — one of
the most fundamental techniques in mathematics. It's especially
useful when you need to extract positive information from negative
hypotheses.

In the next level, you'll use `by_contra` inside a more complex proof:
the double set difference identity.
"

NewTactic by_contra

=== L20_ComplementViaSdiff.lean ===
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 20

Title "Complement via Set Difference"

Introduction "
# Double Set Difference and Complement

Here's a useful identity that connects set difference to intersection:

$$s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$$

Read it aloud: 'removing from `s` everything that's in `s` but not in `t`
leaves exactly the elements of `s` that *are* in `t`.'

This is the **complement principle**: if you think of `s` as a 'universe,'
then `s \\\\ t` is the 'complement of `t` within `s`.' Taking the complement
again (`s \\\\ (s \\\\ t)`) gets you back to `s ∩ t` — the part of `t` inside
your universe.

After `ext x` and rewriting with `Finset.mem_sdiff` (twice) and
`Finset.mem_inter`, the goal becomes:

$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s \\land x \\in t$$

The backward direction is straightforward: if `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`, then
`x ∈ s` is immediate, and `¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)` follows because `x ∈ t`
contradicts `x ∉ t`.

The forward direction needs `by_contra`: you need `x ∈ t`, but your
hypotheses only tell you `x ∈ s` and `¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)`. Assume
`x ∉ t` (using `by_contra`), then construct `⟨h.1, hnt⟩ : x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`,
which contradicts `h.2`.

Note: `simp` is disabled for this level to ensure you practice the
`by_contra` technique manually.

**Your task**: Prove that $s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$.
"

/-- Double set difference recovers intersection: s \ (s \ t) = s ∩ t. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : s \ (s \ t) = s ∩ t := by
  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
  membership biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter]`
  to unfold all operations into logic."
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter]
  Hint "The goal is
  `x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) ↔ x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  -- Forward: x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t) → x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t
  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and
    `h.2 : ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)`. The first conjunct of the goal
    is `h.1`. For the second (`x ∈ t`), use `by_contra`."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `constructor` to split the goal.
    First part: `exact h.1`. Second part: `by_contra hnt`
    introduces `hnt : x ∉ t`, then `exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩`."
    constructor
    · exact h.1
    · Hint "The goal is `x ∈ t`. You can't extract this directly
      from `h`. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x ∉ t` and derive
      a contradiction."
      by_contra hnt
      Hint (hidden := true) "Now `hnt : x ∉ t`. Build the
      contradicting pair: `exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩`."
      exact h.2 ⟨h.1, hnt⟩
  -- Backward: x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ t → x ∈ s ∧ ¬(x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t)
  · Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x ∈ s` and `h.2 : x ∈ t`.
    Use `constructor` to split the goal."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "First part: `exact h.1`. Second part:
    `intro hc; exact hc.2 h.2` — assume `hc : x ∈ s ∧ x ∉ t`,
    then `hc.2 : x ∉ t` contradicts `h.2 : x ∈ t`."
    constructor
    · exact h.1
    · intro hc
      exact hc.2 h.2

Conclusion "
You've proved the **double set difference identity**:
$s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = s \\cap t$.

The key move was `by_contra`: assume `¬(goal)` and derive `False`.
Combined with the negated conjunction hypothesis, this extracted
positive membership information from a double negation.

**The complement perspective**: Think of `s` as a 'universe.' Then:
- `s \\\\ t` is the 'complement of `t` within `s`' — everything in
  the universe that isn't in `t`
- `s \\\\ (s \\\\ t)` is the 'complement of the complement' — which
  gives back `s ∩ t`

This double-complement pattern is exactly the set-theoretic version
of double negation: $\\neg\\neg P \\iff P$. It previews the
**inclusion-exclusion** reasoning you'll use in the Cardinality world,
where counting elements 'not in the complement' is a key technique.
"
